title: 2026 Hot Topics Skill Check
tagline: Storage Lens & FinOps, Iceberg catalogs, partitioning, skew, streaming, CDC, reliability, security, lineage, metadata, event-driven, vector infra and data mesh

questions.prompt: What does AWS S3 Storage Lens primarily give you?
options: A SQL query engine over objects in S3
Org-wide visibility into storage usage and activity across accounts, buckets and prefixes
A service that automatically deletes cold data
A managed Spark cluster for ETL
questions.explanation: Storage Lens aggregates usage and activity metrics across the whole organization (the advanced tier adds prefix-level detail and recommendations). It is the visibility layer — lifecycle policies do the actual transitions/deletes.

questions.prompt: What is the main trade-off of S3 Glacier Deep Archive versus S3 Standard?
options: It is faster to read but more expensive
It is far cheaper per GB but has multi-hour retrieval and a long minimum-storage charge
It has identical retrieval latency but no durability guarantee
It is only available for objects under 1 MB
questions.explanation: Deep Archive is roughly 20x+ cheaper than Standard but has retrieval times measured in hours and a 180-day minimum-storage duration. That is why you only transition genuinely cold data there, and why transitioning millions of tiny objects can cost more than it saves.

questions.prompt: Which of these are genuine storage-waste categories a FinOps review surfaces? (Select all that apply)
options: Incomplete multipart uploads that were never aborted
Non-current object versions in a versioned bucket
Orphaned objects no table or job references
Any object currently stored in S3 Standard
questions.explanation: Incomplete multipart uploads, non-current versions, and orphaned/uncompressed data are real waste you can lifecycle away. Simply being in S3 Standard is not waste — hot, frequently-read data belongs there.

questions.prompt: In Apache Iceberg, what is the correct top-down order of the metadata tree a query walks?
options: data files -> manifest files -> manifest list -> metadata.json
metadata.json -> manifest list -> manifest files -> data files
catalog -> data files -> snapshots -> manifests
manifest list -> metadata.json -> data files -> manifests
questions.explanation: A query resolves the current snapshot in metadata.json, reads the manifest list, prunes to the manifest files whose partition/column stats can match, and only then reads surviving data files. Pruning happens at planning time, before data I/O.

questions.prompt: Now that Iceberg has effectively won the table-format war, where has the senior-interview focus moved?
options: To choosing between CSV and JSON
To the catalog and metadata layer — REST catalogs, planning efficiency and governance
To deciding whether to use a database at all
To row-by-row OLTP tuning
questions.explanation: With the format settled, differentiation and the hard problems moved up the stack to the catalog (Polaris, Unity, Glue, Nessie), metadata/planning efficiency, and governance — the real 2026 battleground.

questions.prompt: What distinguishes Project Nessie among Iceberg catalogs?
options: It is the only catalog that supports Parquet
It adds git-like branches and tags for data, enabling isolated writes and rollbacks
It runs queries itself instead of an engine
It stores data in a relational OLTP database
questions.explanation: Nessie brings version-control semantics (branches, tags, commits) to the catalog, so you can write on a branch, validate, and merge or roll back atomically.

questions.prompt: What is the most common consequence of partitioning a table by a high-cardinality column (e.g., user_id)?
options: Faster writes with no downside
The small-file problem — millions of tiny partitions that wreck planning and read performance
Automatic data compression
Stronger ACID guarantees
questions.explanation: Over-partitioning explodes the number of partitions and files. Per-file overhead then dominates: planning lists millions of files and reads tiny footers. Partition on low-cardinality, frequently-filtered columns (usually date).

questions.prompt: What does Iceberg 'hidden partitioning' provide?
options: It hides the table from unauthorized users
Partition values are derived via transforms (e.g., day(ts)) so queries need no partition column, and partitioning can evolve without rewriting data
It encrypts the partition column
It removes the need for any metadata
questions.explanation: Iceberg stores transform-based partitioning (day(ts), bucket(N, id)) so queries filter on the natural column and still prune. Because partitioning is logical, you can evolve the scheme with no full-table rewrite.

questions.prompt: Z-ordering (or clustering) a table by its common filter columns improves performance by:
options: Compressing the data more aggressively
Physically co-locating related values so per-file min/max stats can skip non-matching files for multi-dimensional filters
Adding a secondary index to every row
Converting the table to row-oriented storage
questions.explanation: Data skipping only works if matching rows are physically clustered. Z-order/clustering narrows each file's value range so min/max stats prune files for filters on the clustered columns.

questions.prompt: A Spark stage has one task running for hours while the rest finished in seconds, because one key owns most rows. What is the right fix?
options: Add more shuffle partitions globally
Salt the hot key (and replicate the small side) or enable AQE skew-join handling
Increase the driver memory
Switch the output format to CSV
questions.explanation: Salting splits the hot key across N partitions; AQE skew-join splits oversized partitions at runtime. Adding partitions globally does not help — the hot key still hashes to one partition.

questions.prompt: Which phrase best captures the nature of a data-skew problem?
options: There is simply too much total data to process
The total work is reasonable, but its distribution across tasks is not
The cluster has too few CPUs
The query returns the wrong answer
questions.explanation: Skew is a distribution problem: a few hot keys do most of the work, so one straggler holds the whole stage hostage even though aggregate work is fine.

questions.prompt: Why is choosing a low-cardinality Kafka partition key (e.g., country) usually a mistake at high throughput?
options: It breaks exactly-once semantics
Popular key values create hot partitions that overload one consumer and cause lag
It prevents compression
Kafka does not allow string keys
questions.explanation: Same-key rows go to the same partition. A skewed low-cardinality key sends a disproportionate share to one partition, overloading its consumer and building lag. Prefer a high-cardinality, evenly-hashed key (e.g., hash(device_id)).

questions.prompt: What ordering guarantee does Kafka provide?
options: Global total ordering across the whole topic
Ordering within a single partition (i.e., per key), not across partitions
No ordering guarantees at all
Ordering only if there is exactly one consumer
questions.explanation: Kafka guarantees order within a partition. Keying by the entity (e.g., primary key) gives per-entity ordering; there is no global order across partitions, which is the trade-off against parallelism.

questions.prompt: Under a schema registry's BACKWARD compatibility mode, which deployment order is safe?
options: Upgrade producers first; consumers cannot read the new data otherwise
Upgrade consumers first — a consumer on the new schema can read data written with the old schema
Order does not matter under any compatibility mode
You must upgrade producers and consumers in the exact same instant
questions.explanation: BACKWARD means the new schema can read data produced with the previous schema, so you upgrade consumers first. (FORWARD is the mirror image — upgrade producers first.) Knowing the direction in deploy-order terms is the senior tell.

questions.prompt: Why is Iceberg schema evolution (add/drop/rename/reorder columns) safe where Hive/positional Parquet schemas break?
options: Iceberg rewrites the whole table on every change
Iceberg tracks columns by unique field IDs, not by position or name
Iceberg disallows dropping columns
Iceberg stores data as JSON
questions.explanation: Iceberg assigns each column a stable field ID, so renames/reorders/drops are metadata-only and never silently misread data — unlike position-based schemas that corrupt on a column reorder.

questions.prompt: What is a data contract?
options: A dashboard test that runs after data lands
A versioned producer/consumer agreement (schema + semantics + SLAs) enforced at the source, typically in CI
A legal document signed by the data team
A backup-and-restore policy
questions.explanation: A data contract shifts quality enforcement left: the producer agrees to a versioned schema/semantics/SLA, enforced in their CI so a breaking change is caught in the PR rather than downstream.

questions.prompt: What does a 'circuit breaker' assertion do in an ingestion pipeline?
options: It speeds up the pipeline by skipping checks
On a failed quality check it stops the run and quarantines the bad batch so it does not propagate downstream
It restarts the database
It silently drops the failing rows and continues
questions.explanation: Tools like Great Expectations, Soda and dbt tests act as circuit breakers — fail fast and dead-letter/quarantine the batch rather than poisoning every downstream consumer.

questions.prompt: Which of these are commonly cited pillars of data observability? (Select all that apply)
options: Freshness
Volume
Schema and distribution
Lineage
questions.explanation: The five data-observability pillars are freshness, volume, schema, distribution, and lineage — the dimensions that tell you a dataset has silently broken.

questions.prompt: Why is column-level lineage preferred over table-level for real impact analysis?
options: It uses less storage
It precisely shows which downstream fields depend on a given column, so you know exactly what breaks if it changes
It is required by SQL
Table-level lineage is always wrong
questions.explanation: Table-level lineage is too coarse — a change to one column may affect only some downstream fields. Column-level lineage gives accurate blast-radius for change management and root-cause.

questions.prompt: What is the main value of OpenLineage / OpenTelemetry in a data platform?
options: They are proprietary tools that lock you into one vendor
Vendor-neutral, standardized lineage and telemetry that every tool in the stack can emit and consume
They replace the need for a catalog
They are only for application performance monitoring, not data
questions.explanation: The shift in 2026 is toward open, vendor-neutral standards so lineage and observability are one consistent signal across orchestrators, engines and BI — not N disconnected tools.

questions.prompt: Why can't you pre-compute a daily 'distinct users' integer and sum 30 of them to get monthly distinct users?
options: Because integers overflow
Because distinct counts are not additive — a user active on multiple days would be counted multiple times
Because daily tables are deleted
Because SUM is slower than COUNT
questions.explanation: Distinct counts are non-additive. To keep roll-ups correct and cheap, store a mergeable HyperLogLog sketch per day and union sketches for any window — never sum pre-computed distinct integers.

questions.prompt: What is the primary job of a feature store?
options: To train the model
To provide online/offline parity and point-in-time-correct features, preventing training/serving skew
To store raw logs cheaply
To serve the final LLM prompt
questions.explanation: A feature store guarantees the features used at serving match those used in training (point-in-time correctness), eliminating training/serving skew — the most common reason a well-tested model fails in production.

questions.prompt: From a data-engineering viewpoint, RAG answer quality depends most on:
options: The cleverness of the prompt alone
The freshness, quality and lineage of the data that is indexed and retrieved
The size of the GPU
The programming language used
questions.explanation: Retrieval is only as good as what's in the index. Freshness (re-embedding on change), quality (contracts) and lineage (doc to chunk to answer) are the levers a data engineer owns — not the prompt.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between RPO and RTO?
options: RPO is recovery time; RTO is data loss
RPO is the acceptable amount of data loss (time window); RTO is the acceptable time to recover service
They are two names for the same metric
RPO applies to storage, RTO applies to networking
questions.explanation: RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data you can afford to lose, set by replication lag. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long recovery may take. Both must be agreed before choosing a DR topology.

questions.prompt: In a lakehouse, what is usually the hardest part of recovering after a region outage?
options: Re-uploading all the Parquet files by hand
Recovering the catalog/metadata consistently — the data files themselves replicate cheaply via cross-region replication
Rewriting every query
Choosing a new file format
questions.explanation: Cross-region replication handles the data files. The blocker is a consistent catalog (Glue/Nessie/Polaris) pointing at them — DR for a lakehouse is mostly a metadata-recovery problem.

questions.prompt: How do active-active and active-passive multi-region designs differ?
options: Active-active is always cheaper
Active-active has both regions serving traffic (needing conflict resolution); active-passive keeps one warm standby with a slower failover
Active-passive serves from both regions simultaneously
They are identical except for naming
questions.explanation: Active-active gives fast failover but requires conflict resolution / single-writer-per-key; active-passive is simpler and cheaper but has a longer RTO while you promote the standby.

questions.prompt: A growing share of 2026 lakehouse production incidents are best described as:
options: Compute failures from slow CPUs
Metadata failures — unmaintained tables with too many snapshots, manifests and small files that strangle planning
Network outages only
Bugs in the SQL parser
questions.explanation: Every write adds snapshots, manifests and files. Without scheduled maintenance, planning time and storage creep up until the table itself becomes the incident — a metadata failure, not a compute one.

questions.prompt: Which are standard Iceberg table-maintenance operations? (Select all that apply)
options: Compaction (rewrite_data_files)
Expiring old snapshots
Rewriting/compacting manifests
Removing orphan files
questions.explanation: Compaction, snapshot expiration, manifest rewrites and orphan-file removal are the core maintenance jobs that keep planning fast and storage lean. Automate them as policy-as-code on a schedule.

questions.prompt: What is an 'error budget' in data platform reliability engineering?
options: The money allocated to fix bugs
The allowed amount of unreliability (1 minus the SLO); burning it gates risky changes
The number of engineers on call
The maximum file size allowed
questions.explanation: If the freshness SLO is 99%, the error budget is the remaining 1%. Spend it down and you freeze risky releases until reliability recovers — the SRE mechanism applied to pipelines.

questions.prompt: Best senior answer to 'what happens if Kafka is unavailable for 4 hours?'
options: All data is permanently lost
If retention exceeds the outage, consumers replay from their last committed offsets on recovery — recoverable with zero data loss but an SLO breach to account for
The pipeline must be rebuilt from scratch
Nothing — Kafka can never go down
questions.explanation: With sufficient retention, consumers resume from committed offsets and replay the backlog; you quantify the freshness SLO breach and error-budget spend. The senior signal is concrete numbers, not 'it depends'.

questions.prompt: How do you honor a GDPR 'right to be forgotten' delete on an immutable lakehouse?
options: You cannot — immutable means data can never be removed
Issue row-level deletes and then compact/rewrite the affected files so the data is physically purged, not just hidden
Drop the entire table
Add a WHERE clause to every query to exclude the user
questions.explanation: Formats like Iceberg/Delta support row-level deletes; the key is to then rewrite/compact the affected files so the bytes are actually gone. Hiding rows with a filter does not satisfy deletion requirements.

questions.prompt: What is dynamic data masking?
options: Encrypting the whole table at rest
Masking sensitive column values at query time based on the role of the user running the query
Deleting sensitive columns permanently
Compressing PII columns
questions.explanation: Dynamic masking applies at read time per role: an analyst sees ***-**-1234 while an authorized role sees the full value — enforced centrally as policy-as-code rather than copied into every query.

questions.prompt: Which describes the canonical modern CDC architecture replacing nightly batch ETL?
options: Spreadsheet exports emailed nightly
OLTP -> log-based CDC (Debezium) -> Kafka -> MERGE/upsert into Iceberg -> Trino
OLTP -> full table dump -> rebuild warehouse from scratch daily
OLAP -> CDC -> OLTP
questions.explanation: Log-based CDC streams every change with low latency: Debezium reads the WAL/binlog, publishes to Kafka, a processor merges into Iceberg, and Trino queries it — a continuous mirror instead of batch.

questions.prompt: How do you make CDC upserts into Iceberg safe against replays and out-of-order events (exactly-once apply)?
options: Hope events never arrive out of order
Make the apply idempotent: upsert keyed on the primary key and dedup/order on a monotonic op_seq or LSN
Disable retries entirely
Use INSERT only and never update
questions.explanation: Kafka only orders within a key, so you key by primary key and make the MERGE idempotent with a monotonic tiebreaker (op_seq/LSN). Replays and late events then cannot corrupt the current state.

questions.prompt: For an interruption-tolerant batch job, what is usually the single biggest lever to cut compute cost?
options: Rewriting it in a different language
Spot/preemptible instances combined with autoscaling (with an on-demand base for the critical path)
Adding more on-demand nodes permanently
Storing the output as CSV
questions.explanation: Spot/preemptible capacity is dramatically cheaper, and autoscaling removes idle burn. Keep the driver/critical path on on-demand. This typically beats micro-optimizing the transformation.

questions.prompt: What does Spark Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) do at runtime?
options: Nothing — the plan is fixed at compile time
Re-optimizes using real statistics: coalesces shuffle partitions, splits skewed partitions, and can switch join strategies
Automatically writes the SQL for you
Encrypts the shuffle data
questions.explanation: AQE adapts the plan mid-query from actual shuffle sizes — coalescing tiny partitions, splitting skewed ones, and flipping sort-merge to broadcast when the real size allows. It's the zero-code first move for skew and cost.

questions.prompt: What is the central goal of metadata engineering as an emerging specialty?
options: To store as much raw data as possible
To make data discoverable and trustworthy — search/discovery, a business glossary, semantic definitions and clear ownership
To delete unused tables automatically
To replace the query engine
questions.explanation: With thousands of datasets, the bottleneck is finding the right, trustworthy one. Metadata engineering treats the catalog as a product: discovery, glossary, semantic layer, and ownership/SLAs.

questions.prompt: What problem does the transactional outbox pattern solve?
options: Slow database reads
The dual-write inconsistency between updating the database and publishing an event — by writing state and the event in one transaction, then relaying the outbox
Schema evolution
Vector search latency
questions.explanation: Writing to the DB and separately publishing to Kafka can leave them inconsistent on failure. The outbox writes the event into the same transaction as the state change; a relay (often CDC) then publishes it — no lost or phantom events.

questions.prompt: What does CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) separate?
options: Reads and writes — one write model and multiple purpose-built read models/projections
Compute and storage
Producers and the schema registry
Batch and streaming engines
questions.explanation: CQRS splits the write model from many read models, each optimized for its query pattern (search, analytics, cache). Paired with event sourcing, projections are rebuilt by replaying the event log.

questions.prompt: An approximate-nearest-neighbor (ANN) index such as HNSW or IVF trades off:
options: Color depth vs resolution
Recall vs latency vs memory
Rows vs columns
ACID vs BASE
questions.explanation: ANN indexes give up exactness for speed: you tune recall (answer quality) against query latency and memory footprint. Exact nearest-neighbor over millions of vectors is too slow for interactive retrieval.

questions.prompt: What is 'hybrid search' in a retrieval pipeline?
options: Running the query on two clouds at once
Combining vector (semantic) similarity with keyword/BM25 search, then re-ranking, for better relevance
Mixing SQL and Python
Searching both rows and columns
questions.explanation: Pure vector recall misses exact-term matches; pure keyword misses semantics. Hybrid search fuses both and re-ranks the candidates — the standard pattern for production RAG relevance.

questions.prompt: What does 'federated computational governance' mean in a data mesh?
options: A central committee approves every change manually
Global, automated standards (interop, security, quality) combined with local domain autonomy
No governance at all
Each domain invents its own incompatible standards
questions.explanation: Federated computational governance sets global, automatically-enforced rules so domain data products interoperate and stay secure, while leaving day-to-day ownership and modeling to the domains. Without it, mesh just becomes silos.

questions.prompt: What does AI-driven 'test impact analysis' do in a CI pipeline?
options: Runs every test on every commit, twice, for safety
Runs only the tests (and pipeline models) actually affected by the diff, cutting CI time dramatically
Deletes failing tests automatically
Generates random test data
questions.explanation: Test impact analysis maps the change to the tests and downstream models it can affect, so CI runs a small relevant subset instead of the full suite — minutes instead of an hour on large repos.

questions.prompt: What is the recommended way to handle flaky tests so they stop blocking merges?
options: Delete them permanently
Automatically detect and quarantine them (so they don't gate the merge) and fix them on a separate track
Ignore all test failures
Re-run the whole pipeline until it passes
questions.explanation: Flaky-test detection classifies non-deterministic tests and quarantines them so they don't block delivery, while still surfacing them for a dedicated fix — rather than eroding trust by blindly retrying or disabling all gates.

questions.prompt: What characterizes anomaly-gated progressive delivery for a data pipeline?
options: Deploy everything at once and hope for the best
Release to a canary while an AI watches data-quality/freshness/error metrics, auto-promoting on health and auto-rolling-back on regression
Only deploy on Fridays
Skip monitoring once the build passes
questions.explanation: Canary/blue-green delivery limits blast radius; an anomaly detector on output metrics (data quality, freshness, error rate) makes the promote-or-rollback decision automatically — closing the reliability loop (self-healing).

questions.prompt: Beyond unit tests, what should CI/CD for a data platform gate a change on?
options: Only that the code compiles
Data-quality and contract-compatibility checks plus blast-radius from column-level lineage
The number of lines changed
Whether it is merged before noon
questions.explanation: Data CI/CD gates on more than code: contract/schema compatibility, data-quality assertions, and the downstream blast radius (from lineage) of the change. That systems view — not just green unit tests — is the senior signal.

questions.prompt: A runaway agent retry loop burns ~$450K overnight. What monitoring gap most directly allowed it?
options: The team had no latency dashboard
Cost was reviewed at monthly/invoice granularity while the burn happened per-minute — no spend-rate ($/min) alarm or kill switch existed
The agents lacked GPU quota
The model provider had an outage
questions.explanation: Each call was individually cheap and 'correct'; the failure was treating cost as a monthly report instead of a runtime metric. The guardrails are spend-rate alarms in $/minute wired to a per-fleet kill switch, plus hard turn caps and backoff in code.

questions.prompt: During a 20-minute provider brownout, spend spikes ~30x. Retries were x3, queue redelivery x2, tool fan-out x5 — each configured correctly by a different team. What is the lesson?
options: One of the three teams misconfigured their component
Amplification multipliers COMPOUND — the 30x exists only at the system level, so cost blast-radius must be reviewed end-to-end, with retry budgets, circuit breakers and idempotency keys
Autoscaling should never be enabled
Brownouts are too rare to design for
questions.explanation: 3 x 2 x 5 = 30: each default is reasonable in isolation and catastrophic as a product. Retry budgets, circuit breakers, bounded queues/DLQs, cost-aware autoscaler caps and end-to-end idempotency keep a brownout from becoming a 30x bill plus duplicate writes.

questions.prompt: A prompt/tooling change ships through a canary that checks p95 latency and error rate, both green — yet the deploy costs ~$735K extra over the next week. What was missing from the gate?
options: A longer soak time at 5%
Delta cost-per-request (and quality evals) as first-class promote/rollback gates next to latency and errors
More canary traffic percentage
A manual approval step
questions.explanation: In the token era a one-line prompt edit is a pricing change. Tokens-per-request tripled while latency and errors stayed flat, so the canary promoted it. Gate the canary on Delta cost-per-request with auto-rollback on band breach, and alert on hourly budget anomalies.

questions.prompt: Which of these are real cost guardrails for AI systems (not just reporting)? (Select all that apply)
options: Per-conversation/per-tenant token budgets enforced in code, failing closed
Spend-rate alarms in dollars-per-minute wired to kill switches
A model routing cascade — the smallest model that passes the evals
Always using the flagship model so quality is never at risk
questions.explanation: Budgets in code, minute-granularity alarms with kill switches, and eval-gated routing are enforcement. 'Flagship for everything' is the anti-pattern from Overage File 03 — it pays ~8-20x for work a small model passes evals on.

questions.prompt: Your AWS-native company acquires a GCP-native target with a 6 PB lake. What is the senior first move?
options: Bulk-migrate all 6 PB to AWS in the first quarter so there is one platform

Federate first — query the data in place across clouds (cross-cloud query engines / an open table format), migrate only datasets that prove hot, with an egress-priced plan and a dated dual-run shutdown
Keep both stacks forever with no consolidation plan
Re-ingest everything from the original sources instead of migrating
questions.explanation: Data has gravity: bulk egress on 6 PB costs ~$480K per copy before re-runs, and 'consolidate in a quarter' becomes 18 months of dual-run. Federate-before-migrate makes egress a per-dataset decision, while contracts (committed-use true-up) and one governance plane are handled on day one.

questions.prompt: Why does Apache Iceberg specifically reduce the cost of a cross-cloud acquisition integration?
options: Iceberg compresses data better than Parquet
It is an open table format that engines on BOTH clouds can read — one copy of the data serves Spark, Trino, BigQuery and Snowflake without per-engine conversion or forced migration
It automatically replicates data between clouds for free
It eliminates the need for a catalog
questions.explanation: The format war's winner is also the M&A escape hatch: with the acquired lake in an open format, the acquirer's engines can query it in place (or via a shared REST catalog) instead of converting/moving petabytes. Egress becomes a choice driven by access patterns, not a prerequisite.
